About: It is about a landmark decision of the Supreme Court of the United States.
It is about the involuntary deprivation of citizenship.
It is about the U.S. government's attempt to revoke citizenship.
It is about the Citizenship Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution.
It is about a federal law mandating loss of U.S. citizenship for voting in a foreign election.
It is about the overruling of the precedent Perez v. Brownell.
It is about the acceptance of dual citizenship in United States law.
It is about the Bancroft Treaties and their eventual abandonment.
It is about the narrowing of its impact by the case Rogers v. Bellei.
It is about the United States Department of State's revised policies in 1990.
Affects: It affects citizens of the United States.
It affects the Supreme Court of the United States.
It affects the United States Department of State.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with Beys Afroyim.
It is associated with the Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution.
It is associated with the Citizenship Clause.
It is associated with Perez v. Brownell.
It is associated with Rogers v. Bellei.
It is associated with the Bancroft Treaties.
It is associated with the Carter administration.
It is associated with the Expatriation Act of 1868.
It is associated with the Nationality Act of 1940.
It is associated with the Supreme Court of the United States.
BelongsTo: It belongs to the history of United States citizenship law.
CausedBy: It is caused by the U.S. government's attempt to revoke the citizenship of Beys Afroyim.
It is caused by Beys Afroyim casting a vote in an Israeli election after becoming a naturalized U.S. citizen.
ConsistsOf: It consists of a Supreme Court ruling.
It consists of a legal argument based on the Fourteenth Amendment.
It consists of the overruling of a previous Supreme Court precedent.
HasA: It has implications for dual citizenship.
It has a connection to the Citizenship Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
It has led to the abandonment of the Bancroft Treaties.
It has been subject to later court decisions that narrowed its impact.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by its ruling that U.S. citizens cannot be involuntarily deprived of citizenship.
It is characterized by its reliance on the Fourteenth Amendment.
It is characterized by its reversal of a prior Supreme Court decision.
It is characterized by its role in the acceptance of dual citizenship.
It is characterized by its impact on treaties limiting dual citizenship.
Influenced: It influenced the acceptance of dual citizenship in United States law.
It influenced the abandonment of the Bancroft Treaties.
It influenced subsequent Supreme Court decisions regarding citizenship.
It influenced revised policies adopted by the United States Department of State in 1990.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by the Citizenship Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
It was influenced by the case Perez v. Brownell (1958).
It was influenced by the case Kennedy v. Mendoza-Martinez (1963).
It was influenced by the case Schneider v. Rusk (1964).
IsA: It is a landmark decision of the Supreme Court of the United States.
It is a ruling that citizens may not be deprived of their citizenship involuntarily.
It is a Supreme Court decision that struck down a federal law.
It is a decision that opened the way for wider acceptance of dual citizenship.
It is a Supreme Court decision that affirmed the right to retain citizenship.
KnownAs: It is known as Afroyim v. Rusk.
KnownFor: It is known for ruling that U.S. citizens cannot be involuntarily deprived of their citizenship.
It is known for its interpretation of the Citizenship Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
It is known for overruling the precedent set in Perez v. Brownell.
It is known for paving the way for dual citizenship in the United States.
It is known for its impact on the Bancroft Treaties.
PartOf: It is part of the legal history of the United States.
It is part of the jurisprudence concerning citizenship.
It is part of the Supreme Court's landmark decisions.
UsedIn: It is used in discussions of citizenship law.
It is used in legal arguments regarding the Fourteenth Amendment.
It is used in understanding the evolution of dual citizenship in the U.S.
UsedTo: It is used to assert that citizens may not be deprived of their citizenship involuntarily.
It is used to strike down federal laws that mandate loss of citizenship for voting in a foreign election.
It is used to support the wider acceptance of dual citizenship.
It is used to argue against the enforceability of treaties limiting dual citizenship.
